codeReview.summary: The fix introduces the right idea — two pending-state variables to capture the pre-clear token before handleInput() clears the store — and the tests now correctly assert swap behavior (From=USDC+To=WETH, select From=WETH → To becomes USDC). However, there is a critical bug in the capture logic that causes the swap to silently degrade to a 'clear' whenever the user types more than one character before clicking the autocomplete option. The bug: handleInput() ALWAYS overwrites pendingCurrentSideToken on every keystroke (because pendingSelectedToken is always null during typing), so after the first keystroke clears the store, subsequent keystrokes overwrite pendingCurrentSideToken with null. The tests and browser verification both avoid this scenario by simulating a single input event with the full token name, so they pass. Real multi-character typing (e.g., typing 'W', 'E', 'T', 'H' as separate keystrokes) would silently revert to the old clear behavior.

issues.description: handleInput() unconditionally overwrites pendingCurrentSideToken on every keystroke. The guard condition `pendingSelectedToken === null || ...` is always true during typing because pendingSelectedToken is immediately reset to null inside the same if-block (line ~183). Sequence for typing 'W','E','T','H': (1) 'W' → pendingCurrentSideToken = USDC, formStore.fromToken = null; (2) 'E' → pendingCurrentSideToken = null (store is now null from step 1!); (3-4) same; (5) click dropdown → currentSideToken = null → clear behavior instead of swap. Fix: change the capture guard to `if (pendingCurrentSideToken === null)` so only the first keystroke captures the pre-clear token value. The store-clearing assignment below the guard remains unchanged.

All swap tests use `fireEvent.input(input, { target: { value: 'WETH' } })` which fires a single input event with the complete value. This does not exercise the multi-keystroke path. The bug (above) would only manifest if the test fired multiple input events with incremental values (e.g., 'W', then 'WE', then 'WET', then 'WETH'). The tests are otherwise correct in their assertions — they validate swap, not clear — but they don't provide adequate coverage of real typing interaction.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The handleInput() overwrite-on-every-keystroke pattern is a latent trap: any future state variable that should be captured once before the store is cleared will face the same issue if placed inside the existing always-true guard. The correct pattern is to use a 'capture-once' guard (e.g., `if (pendingFoo === null)`) rather than an always-reset guard. This is not documented in AGENTS.md or the library.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: TokenInput.svelte lines 178-186: the if-block resets pendingSelectedToken to null, making the condition always true. Compare with the correct pattern: `if (pendingCurrentSideToken === null) { pendingCurrentSideToken = ... }`

sharedStateObservations.observation: Both the unit tests and the browser verification (playwright browser_type fills text at once, not char-by-char) tested only the degenerate single-input-event case. The svelte-worker skill and the mission AGENTS.md do not note that autocomplete interaction tests should exercise multi-keystroke scenarios to avoid this class of regression.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Test file lines ~342-360 use single fireEvent.input; handoff interactiveChecks show browser_type filling full 'WETH' in one operation (not slowly:true). The skill verification step says to confirm swap behavior but does not specify that multi-character typing must be tested.

summary: FAIL. The fix correctly refactors selectToken() to write the current side first, then set the other side to the saved token. Tests now assert true swap behavior (To becomes USDC, not null). However, a bug in handleInput() causes pendingCurrentSideToken to be overwritten with null on every keystroke after the first (because pendingSelectedToken is always reset to null in the same guard, making the condition always true). As a result, the swap only works when the user types a single character before clicking — any multi-character typing (the common case) silently reverts to the old clear behavior. Both unit tests and the browser test avoided multi-character typing (single input event / fill-at-once), so neither caught this. Fix: change the capture guard from the always-true condition to `if (pendingCurrentSideToken === null)`.
